coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot dispersal in PVA thin film for food colorant sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Ng Hau Kwan, Melvin
Leo, C.P.
Arosa Senanayake, S.M.N.
Lim, G.K.
Tan, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: Synthetic food colorant is commonly found in food products such as candies, beverages, pastry and dairy products. Excessive consumption of synthetic food colorant especially tartrazine may cause health problems. The discharge of wastewater containing tartrazine from food and beverage plants can further create environmental problems. Precise and rapid monitoring of food colorant during food and beverage processing at low cost is important to avoid these problems. A new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (Cdots) with a size smaller than 10 nm have received great attention in the sensing of food colorant due to their biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity and excellent photostability. The dispersal of Cdots in solid-state thin films is important to further expand and reinforce their practical application. In this work, the dispersal of Cdots in low cost, biocompatible and transparent poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) was studied. Cdots were first synthesized via microwave assisted method before the dispersal in PVA and PVA/PEG solution. PVA/Cdots and PVA/PEG/Cdots thin films were formed using citric acid as the crosslinking agent and curing at 140 °C for 4 h. Cdots in spherical shapes with an average diameter smaller than 10 nm were successfully synthesized as proven in high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) image. The effects of Cdots on thin film properties such as morphology and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were studied. The PL emission spectra of Cdots and PVA/PEG/Cdots thin film was recorded. The composite films showed photoluminescent property with the addition of Cdots. The films showed promising results to be developed as a tartrazine sensor in food quality control as they could detect tartrazine at a concentration as low as 10 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dot
Sensor
PVA
Colorant
Thin film

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green Technology for the Sustainable Tomorrow

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Cdots and Cdots thin film
Characterization of Cdots and Cdots thin films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of Cdots and PVA/Cdots.
PVA/CDOT, PVA/PEG and PVA/PEG/CDOT under normal and UV light.
SEM cross sectional image of (a) PVA/PEG, (b) PVA/CDOT, and (c) PVA/PEG/CDOT thin films.
FTIR spectra for Cdots and polymeric thin films incorporated Cdots.
Fluorescence emission spectra of Cdots solution after quenching with different concentration of tartrazine.
Light transmission (Left) and fluorescence emission spectra (Right) of (a) PVA/CDOT, (b) PVA/PEG, (c) PVA/PEG/CDOT before (dotted line) and after (solid line) adsorption of tartrazine.
Proposed static quenching and IFE mechanisms of Cdots in thin film in the sensing of tartrazine.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot dispersal in PVA thin film for food colorant sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14700, Nibong Tebal, Penang, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Engineering Campus, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 14700 Nibong Tebal, Penang, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, University Brunei Darussalam, Jalan Tungku Link, BE1410, Gadong, Brunei

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 11800 USM, Penang, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Universiti Sains Malaysia, 11800 USM, Penang, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthetic food colorant is commonly found in food products such as candies, beverages, pastry and dairy products. Excessive consumption of synthetic food colorant especially tartrazine may cause health problems. The discharge of wastewater containing tartrazine from food and beverage plants can further create environmental problems. Precise and rapid monitoring of food colorant during food and beverage processing at low cost is important to avoid these problems. A new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (Cdots) with a size smaller than 10 nm have received great attention in the sensing of food colorant due to their biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity and excellent photostability. The dispersal of Cdots in solid-state thin films is important to further expand and reinforce their practical application. In this work, the dispersal of Cdots in low cost, biocompatible and transparent poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) was studied. Cdots were first synthesized via microwave assisted method before the dispersal in PVA and PVA/PEG solution. PVA/Cdots and PVA/PEG/Cdots thin films were formed using citric acid as the crosslinking agent and curing at 140 °C for 4 h. Cdots in spherical shapes with an average diameter smaller than 10 nm were successfully synthesized as proven in high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) image. The effects of Cdots on thin film properties such as morphology and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were studied. The PL emission spectra of Cdots and PVA/PEG/Cdots thin film was recorded. The composite films showed photoluminescent property with the addition of Cdots. The films showed promising results to be developed as a tartrazine sensor in food quality control as they could detect tartrazine at a concentration as low as 10 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Artificial food colorants derived from crude oil have been widely used in food industry due to their low cost and high stability. For instance, tartrazine has been long approved and used as a food additive or oral medication in the European Union (EU) [1]. However, the use of artificial food colorants is now strictly regulated in the EU, US and many other countries due to the adverse side effects. The synthetic food colorant could result in various symptoms, including diarrhea, vomiting, gastric upset, urticarial, angioedema and migraines [2]. As recommended by the experts from WHO and other organizations, the acceptable daily intake (ADI) for tartrazine is 7.5 mg kg−1 body weight [2]. Therefore, it is crucial to detect the presence of tartrazine in food products for monitoring the consumption and protecting the safety of consumer.

To detect tartrazine, several well-established analytical methods are available, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical sensor, capillary electrophoresis and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) and spectrophotometry [2]. Spectrophotometry has gain popularity recently due to the facile methods to synthesis fluorescent nanoparticles, especially carbon dots (Cdots). Cdots not only can be synthesized from abundance and various carbon sources, simple and cheap synthesis processes are also available to obtain satisfactory fluorescent properties [3]. Depending on the size of Cdots, Cdots can emit blue, green, yellow, or orange light while maintaining the same excitation wavelength (λex) [4]. Generally, the synthesis methods can be categorized to be top-down methods or bottom-up approaches [5]. Top down methods involve the breaking down of large carbon molecules into nano Cdots, while bottom-up approaches produce Cdots from precursor. Among the bottom-up approaches method, hydrothermal [6,7] and microwave assisted method [8,9] were commonly employed due to the high quantum yield (˜ 80%) and fast reaction time (few minutes) [5]. For example, Xu et al. [7] produced Cdots from fresh aloe via hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 11 h. The resulted Cdots with spherical shape and an average diameter of 5 nm could emit bright yellow photoluminescence. Similarly, Jiangling He et al. [6] used polyvinyl alcohol as the carbon source and ethanediamine as the nitrogen source to obtain uniform Cdots within the range of 7–9 nm via hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 10 h. The Cdots achieved the maximum emission at 420 nm when they were excited at 340 nm. The size of Cdots were affected by reactant concentration, temperature and time of reaction, additives and etc. It is important to optimize the temperature and time because the fluorescence intensity decreased when these parameters were not properly regulated [7]. On the other hand, Hou et al. [7] synthesized Cdots using microwave assisted method. When the carbon precursor solution either citric acid [7] or triammonium citrate [9] was heated using microwave, partial carbonization occurred after water evaporation. They postulated nucleation initiation during the thermal carbonization of precursor solution. The presence of phosphate as foaming agent not only aided the reaction by accelerating the reaction under ionic conduction effect, but also prevented the non-uniform nucleation growth.

The uses of Cdots as sensor can be divided into 3 different mechanisms, namely (1) direct interaction of analytes, (2) post functionalization of Cdots, and (3) integration of Cdots with quenchers or other substrates [5]. Cdots are substantial sensors since their photoluminescence properties are adjustable using these mechanisms. Various metal ions (Fe3+,Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cr6+, etc.), anions (PO4 3+, S2+, CN−.) and even molecules (thiols, glucose drugs, pesticides, dye, etc.) [4,5] could be detected. Xu et al. [7] used Cdots to detect tartrazine and found that the decline of fluorescence intensity in the linear range, extending from 0.25 to 32.50 μM. They also determined the presence of tartrazine in food samples such as buns, honey and candy collected from local market. Later on, the same research group [10] was able to detect the presence of tartazine in orange juice, apple juice and honey using 3-mercapto-1,2,4-triazolecapped gold nanoclusters which generated comparable results to HPLC. These findings suggested that fluorescent Cdots have great potential toward food analysis. However, the major disadvantages of Cdots nanoparticles include sample handling and recovery. Hence, the immobilization of Cdots into different substrates without losing its photoluminescence properties was important for future applications. Recently, Wang et al. [11] immobilized Cdots in the molecularly imprinted microgels (poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-acrylamide-vinylphenylboronic acid) via one pot synthesis for optical sensing of glucose. The fluorescence signals of the embedded Cdots can be quenched and recovered as the hybrid microgels could swell reversibly sand shrink in response to the variation of surrounding glucose concentration. Others researchers tried to immobilize Cdots on PVA via electrospinning [6] or simple blending method [12]. According to He et al. [6], the ordered mesoporous structure formed by the electrospun Cdots/PVA nanofibers could stabilize the triplet states of Cdots effectively, the nanofibers show a long average afterglow lifetime of 1.61 s with visually recognizable period of 9 s.

In this work, Cdots were synthesized using microwave assisted method. The Cdots were further blended into PVA/PEG polymer dope solution. PVA and PEG polymer were chosen because they are hydrophilic and transparent. These properties are important to minimize the hindrance to the excitation and emission of Cdots. The polymer dope solution was then cast into thin films with superior adsorption properties. The thin films were characterized by spectrophotometer to confirm the presence of Cdots in the films. The adsorption of tartarzine using these thin films was studied. Unlike others, the effects of embedment on the sensing properties of Cdots were further reported.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA with average molecular weight of 31,000 to 50,000 Da, 87–89 % hydrolyzed), polyethylene glycol (PEG, with average molecular weight of 10,000 Da) and tartrazine (purity > 85%) were acquired from Sigma, while methanol (absolute) and citric acid (CA, ≥ 99%) were supplied by Merck. K2HPO4 was supplied by R & M chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cdots and Cdots thin film
ce:section.ce:para: Cdots were synthesized using microwave assisted method. The carbon source, citric acid was dissolved together with K2HPO4 in distilled water at a mass ratio of 5:1:10 as reported elsewhere [8]. The precursor solution as much as 10 mL was stirred using a magnetic stirrer (WiseStir MSH – 20D) at 300 rpm for 30 min, then it was heated in microwave (SHARP) at 750 W for 150 s. The resulting solid was dispersed in distilled water (50 mL) by stirring for another 1 h at 300 rpm. Suspended solid was removed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 30 min and the supernatant were further purified using 0.45 μm syringe filter to remove large agglomerate.

The dope solution of polymeric thin film was prepared as followed. PVA was added slowly into the Cdots solutions at 500 rpm and 90 °C under stirring till it was completely dissolved. PEG was then added as a co-polymer. The cross-linker, CA was added into the mixture which was continuously stirred for 3 h. The weight ratio of PVA:CA was maintained at 60:40. The dope solutions of various thin films were prepared based on the composition listed in Table 1 . The dope solution was degassed using a sonicator (Elmansonic, S80 H) for 30 min and further stirred at 80 rpm to eliminate air bubble and sedimentation.

Polymer thin films were prepared from these dope solutions. 5 g of dope solution were poured on the Petri dish. The polymer thin films were dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight to remove the water. The thin films were peeled off and cured at 140 °C for 4 h. The cured thin films were then cut into circular samples with a diameter of 2 cm. The sensing properties of Cdots films were tested in term of tartrazine adsorption using 10 mL of tartrazine solution at 10 μM for 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Cdots and Cdots thin films
ce:section.ce:para:
The Cdots samples were dropped on a copper grid coated with holey carbon film. The solvent was evaporated and the microstructure of Cdots was observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 kV with Field Emission, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, FEI). The average thickness of the films was measured using a thickness gauge (Mitutoyo, 547-400S). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Cdots and thin films were collected using a FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, Nicolet iS10) in the range of 4000–650 cm−1. The percentage transmittance of light (200–1000 nm) through the thin films was measured using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Carry 60, Agilent). The morphology of the surface and cross-section of thin films were studied using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 200 FESEM, FEI). The thin films were cracked using liquid N2 for cross-sectional view. The fluorescence properties of the Cdots were determined using fluorescence spectrometer. (PerkinElmer LS55). The excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm, the widths of excitation and emission were both 15 nm with a scan speed of 1000 nm per min. The Cdots thin film were measured similarly, with the aid of solid sample holder accessory with 1% light transmittance setting. The limit of detection (LOD) was also calculated as followed.
(1) LOD = 3σ/K
where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 5) and K is the slope of the calibration graph (Fig S1).

In consistent with other works [4,11,13–15], Cdots in spherical shapes with an average diameter smaller than 10 nm were successfully synthesized as proven by HRTEM image presented in Fig. 1 . The Cdots appeared to be stable as minimum agglomeration was observed. When the Cdots solution was exposed to a light beam at 350 nm, the electrons of Cdots was excited. The Cdots emitted light around 430 nm when the electrons lost their energy and return to ground states. The extracted Cdots at high concentration emitted extremely high fluorescent intensity which was unmeasurable. With a dilution factor of 1000 times in distilled water, the Cdots showed high fluorescent intensity as recorded in Fig. 2 . It still showed emission even after dilution at 8000 times. The fluorescent intensity of the Cdots was also proportional to the concentration of Cdots. After blending Cdots into the polymeric dope solution, the fluorescent property of Cdots remained in the composite films as observed under UV irradiation (Fig. 3 ). Liu et al. [14] also reported on the similar observation that carbon dots nanoparticles exhibited fluorescence emissions after dispersed into PVA. Liu et al. [14] reported on the increment of fluorescence quantum yields of Cdots in PVA thin film due to improvement of surface passivation effects. By varying the precursor of Cdots in one-pot-synthesis, Bhunia et al. [16] could even synthesized Cdots-polydimethylsiloxane thin films with the emission of different colors.

The thickness of PVA and PVA/PEG films are recorded to be similar, namely 146 ± 8.38 μm and 145 ± 10.92 μm. With the presence of Cdots, the thickness of both PVA/Cdots and PVA/PEG/Cdots thin films increased slightly up to 155 ± 7.12 μm and 154 ± 12.0 μm, respectively. To further understand the morphology of the thin films, SEM was used to scan the membrane cross-section. The SEM images are presented in Fig. 4 . All the thin films fabricated in this work showed dense and non-porous structure. PVA and PEG are compatible with each other, forming homogeneous structure. The addition of Cdots did not create any defects in the thin films, showing their compatibility to PVA and PEG as well. The finding is consistent with the past study [17] that reported on the dense structure of epoxy thin film incorporated with Cdots. A recent study by Wang et al. [18] also described the formation of defect-free dense structure when Cdots were embedded into polyvinylpyrrolidone thin film. Wang et al. [18] studied on the application of Cdots as phosphors in a solar concentrator without sensing properties.

The most abundant functional group in PVA, OH was noticeable from the broad peak occurred in the range of 3700–3000 cm−1 as shown in FTIR spectra (Fig. 5 ). Sp3 hybridized of aromatic CH stretching vibrations was confirmed by the existence of a small peak around 3000–2900 cm−1. Other noticeable peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1150 to 1300 cm−1 was attributable to CO stretching vibration of ester bond and carboxyl CO groups and symmetric CC stretching, respectively. Although Cdots nanoparticles were incorporated into PVA and PVA/PEG thin films, they exhibited similar characteristic peaks in FTIR spectra because the characteristics peaks of PVA overlapped the characteristics peaks of Cdots. The presence of PEG added an additional peak at 1640 cm−1, which indicates the CO stretching of the ester group formed between PVA and PEG [19].

Before the sensing properties of thin films were tested, Cdots were first mixed with different concentration of tartrazine to confirm the sensing properties of Cdots. In Fig. 6 , it was observed that the Cdots lost their fluorescence after the addition of tartrazine. The Cdots were no longer fluorescent when a high concentration of tartrazine (> 1000 μM) was present in the sample. The high sensitiveness of Cdots to detect tartrazine in a low and wide range of concentration (1–1000 μM) showed promising results as a sensor. Xu et al. [7] suggested the presence of tartrazine could reduce the fluorescence of carbon dot by static quenching. This is because Stern−Volmer quenching constant was inversely correlated with temperature and there was a shift in the absorption spectrum, indicating a formation of ground state complex. Theodoros et al. [20] agreed that the quenching mechanisms fall in a regime of static quenching at a low concentration of tartrazine. In addition, inner filter effect (IFE) may also contributed to the ‘quenching’ effect as there is an overlapping of emission wavelength (around 430 nm) of Cdots and the absorption wavelength of tartrazine (λmax =430 nm) [20].

Fluorescent Cdots can be easily synthesized using microwave assisted hydrothermal method. The synthesized Cdots were successfully immobilized in PVA and PVA/PEG thin films as shown by fluorescence emission spectra. The composite films showed photoluminescent property with the addition of Cdots. The films showed promising results to be developed as a tartrazine sensor in food quality control as they could detect tartrazine at a concentration as low as 10 μM. The selectively of these sensing thin films should be further studied using interfering compounds before testing on the real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge The Royal Society for supporting this research (Commonwealth Science Conference Follow-on Travel Grant 2017). Universiti Sains Malaysia is acknowledged for providing Graduate Assistant Scheme to Mr. Melvin Ng Hau Kwan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2019.103187.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New considerations regarding the risk assessment on Tartrazine: an update toxicological assessment, intolerance reactions and maximum theoretical daily intake in France
Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol.
